melancholic_introspection: “Got it.” Only five minutes or so later, Arnold’s breathing slowed. After ensuring he was truly asleep, Rishe removed her hand. Then she brushed her fingers against Arnold’s lips. There was that ache in her chest again.
As we sat in silence with our legs sprawled, the lunch bell rang, echoing through the otherwise-empty gym. My stomach rumbled in agreement.

After the incident the day before, I was kind of worried. I mean, I was probably just overthinking it, but still. What if she stopped coming to the loft over the encounter? I would regret it for the rest of…maybe not my life , but the semester, at the very least. Once the next semester started, I’d

Deep down on the inside, I was riddled with holes. Holes where my heart should have been. Maybe it was just my empty stomach talking, but right then, the holes were screaming at me, telling me that something was missing.
The empty space between her and me drove me bonkers . I was torn between the urge to say something—anything—and the desire to flee. Since I couldn’t think of anything to say, I chose the latter.

“Nice to meet you,” Adachi finished curtly, then leaned back against the net and stared silently at the opposite wall. She radiated such an intense “stay away” vibe that neither Hino nor Nagafuji had the courage to engage with her.

I sat cross-legged in the center of the room, wearing my swimsuit. Every time I tried to open my mouth, sweat trickled down my back. Happy 8th birthday, Ann. Many sad things might have happened. There might be several others to work hard on. But do not give in. Although you might be lonely and crying desolately, do not forget: Mom will always love Ann.

“I wonder… if you’re… lonely… that I’m not there. romantic_tension: From the looks of it, some girls in our grade were setting up volleyball courts for gym class. I only knew they were in our grade because I spotted my friends, Hino and Nagafuji, carrying the poles and net. If they walked onto the stage and looked up at the second floor, they would see us… My heart

Was she mad at me for doing that? I glanced back to check, and our eyes met again, so I averted my gaze. Oh my god, why is this so awkward? It felt like we were a couple of twelve-year-olds trying to hide our relationship from the rest of the school… Then again, I suppose that, in a sense, we kind o

As we walked in silence, I snuck a few glances at her. In profile, her hair lay perfectly against her high cheekbones, as though she were a carefully crafted statue. After a moment, she blinked, and I was relieved to remember that she was a living, breathing human being. Then our eyes met, and I rea
Lastly, a younger girl—almost certainly the sister she’d mentioned last time we hung out—stood in Shimamura’s shadow. Our eyes met. She seemed shyly interested in my cheongsam .
At that thought, I turned slightly, and Shimamura entered my field of vision, her face mere inches from mine. Just like in the dream. Then our eyes met.
And it just kept going. I lay there in bed with headphones on in the dark, dripping sweat as my heart pounded away. She was trying to hypnotize me!
She did not understand very well why her heart beat so rapidly, but it was because she had caught a glimpse of a woman’s adult side from Violet.
comedic_timing: F ROM THE MOMENT Adachi first suggested that we play ping-pong, a hot new fad was born—under the radar, of course, since we were skipping class. The school ping-pong equipment had been left to gather dust on the second floor of the gym, and because we couldn’t risk opening the window, the air was st
She often wore a bracelet on her left wrist—an oversized bangle, reminiscent of a single handcuff, that always looked as though it was trying to slip off.

Make no mistake, however—Adachi was easily three times the delinquent I was. One look at her attendance record would prove that. But what pissed me off was that, even if I were three times better than her, I would never be seen as a “good” student. Yet, somehow, she still managed to get the same gra

And so, we idled away the time playing ping-pong—casual games, for the most part, though we had our moments of intense competition. Meanwhile, the rest of the school sat through third period. What was third period on Mondays again? Math? History? I tried to remember, but most of my brainpower was fo
To be clear, Adachi and I hadn’t randomly decided to start playing hooky together. We started out doing our own separate things—and, frankly, Adachi didn’t come to school that often to begin with.

For some reason, her shoulders flinched in response. “Uhh…yeah. A snack.” “What, so a non-sugary snack?” “No, no, sugary’s good,” she answered in a tiny voice that I could barely make out through her pouted lips. I’d tried dozens of times to “casually” steer the conversation, only to end up in a panic—I just wasn’t capable of keeping my cool. Instead, I shook like a leaf until my desired
“Oh, uh, on second thought, actually, I don’t mind.” I nearly added “as long as you’re there,” but hastily choked it back down. Can’t say that in front of the others—I’d better watch my mouth.

“Shima-chan?” she asked incredulously. That was my name, all right—and a rather affectionate nickname at that. My sight went blurry, and my brain numbed as it struggled to process the situation. The only people who ever called me “Shima-chan” were friends from elementary school. Mentally, I compared

“Oh, I get it. You used too much brainpower during class, so now you’re filling up on sugar?” “Uh…yeah…something like that,” Adachi replied instantly—so fast, I wasn’t sure she’d actually listened to a word I said. As she rested her chin in her hands, I noticed a bottle of mineral water on her desk.

Time passed in an astonishingly quick manner as Rishe tried to summon all sorts of resolve. The soft sound of the waves echoed on the beach at dusk. Along the edge of the loft overlooking the gym’s ground floor ran a green net, a relic from the days when this school had an official ping-pong club. The net’s purpose was to ensure that any stray balls remained within the bounds of the second floor. Normally, the two of us sat right against it and

It was right at the start of second semester. When I walked in, Adachi was lounging with her shoes and socks off, probably due to the heat. She must’ve thought I was a teacher at first, because she bolted upright when she saw me, her cute little toes all splayed out. I remember it vividly to this da
After that, we started hanging out more and more frequently. We didn’t schedule it, though. Every now and then, I’d get the feeling she was up in the loft, so I’d come by, and voil à, she’d be there.
“Yeah, I can relate. I’d be out of there so fast,” Adachi agreed, stretching her arms above her head.

“Get good, scrub! Hyah!” Striking a weird pose, she served another ball. This time, she must’ve hit it too low, because it bounced backward and hit the wall behind her. She dashed off to retrieve it, then came back scratching her forehead.
Or perhaps “beating around the bush” was more apt than “convoluted,” considering the lap she’d done around me just now. Guess that makes me the bush.
